nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes it from normal short sleep or transient stress-related poor sleep.

Explains treatment including "CBT-I" as first-line therapy for chronic insomnia and describes at least one CBT-I component (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene).

Before any prescribing, explicitly verifies: medication allergies, current medication/substance use to assess drug interactions (including alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, cannabis, OTC antihistamines), and contraindications (pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment if relevant, respiratory compromise/OSA risk, history of complex sleep behaviors, fall risk).

Screens for secondary causes and red flags: depression/suicidality, mania/hypomania, anxiety/PTSD, substance use disorder, pain, thyroid symptoms, and medications that disrupt sleep (stimulants, steroids, decongestants).

Assesses for comorbid sleep disorders warranting referral: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome (urge to move legs, worse at rest/night), circadian rhythm disorder (shift work, delayed sleep phase), parasomnias.

Characterizes insomnia with clinically relevant details: onset/duration, sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening, frequency, daytime impairment, sleep schedule, naps, caffeine timing, and sleep environment.

If prescribing, recommends the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration, provides counseling on next-day impairment, driving/operating machinery, avoidance of alcohol/CNS depressants, and plans for discontinuation.

Avoids unsafe prescribing (e.g., chronic benzodiazepines or Z-drugs without clear indication/monitoring; sedatives in high-risk patients such as untreated suspected OSA, significant respiratory depression risk, or active substance misuse).

Provides a follow-up plan timeframe (e.g., 2–4 weeks) and measurable goals (sleep diary, ISI score, functional improvement), and states when to seek urgent care (severe mood symptoms, suicidality, dangerous medication effects).

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the terms "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and "genetic," and clarifies it is a hereditary peripheral neuropathy rather than an acute injury or infection.

Describes expected symptom pattern and course (distal weakness/atrophy, sensory loss, foot deformities such as pes cavus/hammer toes, gait instability; typically slowly progressive) and checks functional impact (falls, walking endurance, hand dexterity).

Elicits key history elements relevant to CMT evaluation: family history, age of onset, progression, prior neurologic evaluations/EMG/NCS, and red flags that would suggest alternative diagnoses (rapid progression, prominent autonomic symptoms, asymmetric deficits).
Meets communication milestone explain_treatment by explicitly including "physical therapy" and "orthotics" as core management strategies and sets expectations that supportive care is mainstay.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (critical), screens for contraindications relevant to the chosen medication (critical; e.g., NSAID renal/GI/bleeding risk, pregnancy considerations), and considers drug interactions (noncritical).

Avoids recommending medications known to worsen peripheral neuropathy or that are classically cautioned in CMT without careful risk-benefit discussion (e.g., vincristine and other neurotoxic agents) and advises the patient to inform clinicians of CMT before new medications.
Provides counseling on fall prevention and supportive devices (ankle-foot orthoses, appropriate footwear), and considers occupational therapy for hand weakness and activities of daily living.
Offers genetic counseling/testing discussion and addresses implications for family planning and screening of relatives, respecting patient preferences.
Creates a clear follow-up plan and return precautions (worsening weakness, new rapid decline, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, severe pain, frequent falls).
Uses patient-centered language, checks understanding (teach-back), and documents shared decision-making for referrals and symptom treatment choices.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose CMT, provide symptom-focused medication only if needed with mandatory safety checks, and refer for multidisciplinary supportive care (PT/orthotics) and neurology confirmation/counseling.

distal sensory symptoms (numbness/tingling) or reduced sensation

Discuss treatment emphasizing physical therapy and orthotics/bracing; add fall-prevention and supportive footwear guidance.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia benchmark, the core outcome is recognizing and documenting insomnia as the working diagnosis after basic evaluation and exclusion of obvious secondary causes.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and frames it as a clinical diagnosis based on symptoms and functional impact.

Elicits key insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, frequency (nights/week) and duration (weeks/months), and daytime impairment (fatigue, mood, concentration).

Screens for common contributors/secondary causes appropriate for L1: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol use, shift work/irregular schedule, stress/anxiety/depression symptoms, pain, reflux, nocturia, and medication/substance triggers (e.g., stimulants, steroids, decongestants).
Assesses basic sleep hygiene and sleep schedule: bedtime/wake time, time in bed, naps, screen use, exercise timing, and bedroom environment.

Checks for red flags requiring escalation rather than simple insomnia labeling: symptoms suggestive of obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome, mania/hypomania, suicidality, or severe uncontrolled medical illness.
Provides patient-centered education explaining what insomnia is and how perpetuating factors (conditioned arousal, irregular schedule, time in bed) maintain symptoms.
Explains first-line treatment including CBT-I by name and briefly outlines components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive strategies, relaxation, sleep hygiene).
Discusses melatonin as an option (including typical timing before bedtime and that benefit is modest), and sets expectations about onset and duration of trial.
Avoids initiating prescription hypnotics in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if medications are discussed, emphasizes non-pharmacologic first-line management and short-term/lowest-risk approaches.

If the agent does prescribe in narrative despite L1, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about drug allergies, current medications/OTC/herbals to assess interactions, and contraindications (pregnancy, hepatic disease, sleep apnea risk, substance use disorder risk, older age/fall risk).
Uses clear, empathetic communication, checks understanding, and offers a follow-up timeframe to reassess response and reconsider secondary causes if not improving.
